hypothesis: The dual momentum concept has shown genuine walk-forward consistency across iterations (1.0 consistency score, OOS often > IS Sharpe), but every iteration has failed on either trade count or edge. Root cause analysis across 8 iterations reveals a clear pattern: ANY absolute momentum filter (200-day MA in iter 6, return>0 in iters 7/8) catastrophically suppresses trade count. The fix: REMOVE the absolute momentum filter entirely and always hold the top-N ETFs by relative momentum (pure cross-sectional momentum, no cash allocation). This guarantees minimum N positions at all times, ensuring constant market exposure and maximum trade frequency. Weekly rebalancing (every 5 days) × 8 ETFs × top 3 held = ~52 rebalances/year × ~1.5 position changes per rebalance = ~75-100 trades over 5-year IS period. The 63-day lookback (quarter) is the academically validated momentum window from Jegadeesh & Titman — long enough to capture genuine momentum but short enough to be responsive. Always-invested (no cash) removes regime dependency that caused IS underperformance in iter 5/6/8. The strategy is long-only (avoids short-side losses from iter 4) and holds 3 positions simultaneously (diversification reduces per-position drawdown). By eliminating the absolute filter, we guarantee 50+ trades while relying on relative momentum's well-documented cross-sectional alpha in equity ETFs.

evaluation.post_mortem: This iteration successfully demonstrated genuine walk-forward consistency (1.0 score, 7/7 windows passed) and OOS Sharpe actually exceeds IS Sharpe (0.58 vs 0.47), which is a strong anti-overfitting signal. The strategy also survives transaction costs and has a favorable profit factor (2.59) and reasonable win rate (59%). Removing the absolute momentum filter did fix the directional issue of IS/OOS gap. However, the strategy critically failed on two hard acceptance criteria: (1) Trade count is catastrophically low — only 13.875 IS trades and 8.0 OOS trades vs. the required minimum of 50. The hypothesis that removing the absolute filter would yield 75-100 trades was completely wrong; the pure cross-sectional top-3 approach with weekly rebalancing still generates far too few actual position changes, likely because the top-3 holdings are sticky and rarely change. (2) IS Sharpe of 0.47 is far below the 1.0 threshold, and OOS Sharpe of 0.58 is also well below the 0.7 target. The low trade count undermines statistical significance of any performance metrics. The good news is that the IS/OOS relationship is healthy — this strategy concept is not overfit, it's just too low-frequency and too low-return to meet the bar.
evaluation.next_direction: The core problem is trade count — only ~14 IS trades vs. 50 required. The top-3 holdings from 8 ETFs are too sticky with a 63-day momentum lookback. To dramatically increase trade frequency while preserving the healthy IS/OOS relationship: (1) Expand the universe significantly — use 15-20 ETFs (add sector ETFs: XLK, XLF, XLE, XLV, XLI, XLB, XLU, XLP, XLY, XLRE plus international/factor ETFs) so there are more rank changes per rebalance; (2) Reduce top_n_held to 1 or 2 (fewer held positions = more frequent rank crossovers triggering trades); (3) Try a shorter momentum lookback of 20-30 days (more responsive ranking = more frequent position changes); (4) Consider daily rebalancing (rebalance_days=1) to maximize trade opportunities. The combination of a larger universe + shorter lookback + fewer positions should generate 50+ trades while the proven walk-forward consistency of the momentum concept should be preserved.
